Registry / Terms / noise

noise

—Not in VIM/VIML G 18 #01053 · 6 instances

Suggested actions

  1. HIGH No authoritative definition in VIM or VIML.
  2. MEDIUM 6 distinct definitions — harmonise.

Publication instances

Ed.YearPublicationClauseDefinitionConsistency
20102006OIML R 82:2006
#01053
3.9
Manifestation of variation in the gas chromatograph output signal not associated with changes in chromatographic conditions (e.g. temperature gradient), which can be divided into two components: 3.9.1 Short-term noise, which includes all observable random variations of the signal from the detector or other components having a frequency of the order of one or more cycles per minute. 3.9.2 Drift, i.e. the average slope of the baseline signal measured over a minimum of half an hour
pending
20101994OIML R 112:1994
#01480
2.8
a measure of variation in the detector signal; it can be divided Althoug long-term noise can occur at any time into three components: dur * short-term noise includes all observable random lat variations of the detector signal having a frequency of usu the order of 10–2 Hz to 10–1 Hz (one or more cycles per lon minute) and should be measured peak to peak * long-term noise includes all observable random variations of the detector signal with frequencies between 0.1 Hz and 1.0 Hz * drift is the average slope of the baseline signal measured over a minimum period of one-half hour.
pending
20101994OIML R 113:1994
#01495
3.8
a measure of the variation in the detector's output signal when a sample component is not being detected. This can be classified into two types: * Short-term noise includes all observable random variations of the signal from the detector or other components having a frequency of about one or more cycles per minute. * Drift is the average slope of the baseline signal measured over an extended time period, for example, one-half hour
pending
202X2006OIML R 82:2006
#01053
3.9
Manifestation of variation in the gas chromatograph output signal not associated with changes in chromatographic conditions (e.g. temperature gradient), which can be divided into two components: Short-term noise, which includes all observable random variations of the signal from the detector or other components having a frequency of the order of one or more cycles per minute. Drift, i.e. the average slope of the baseline signal measured over a minimum of half an hour
pending
202X1994OIML R 112:1994
#01480
2.8
a measure of variation in the detector signal; it can be divided into three components: short-term noise includes all observable random variations of the detector signal having a frequency of the order of 10–2 Hz to 10–1 Hz (one or more cycles per minute) and should be measured peak to peak long-term noise includes all observable random variations of the detector signal with frequencies between 0.1 Hz and 1.0 Hz drift is the average slope of the baseline signal measured over a minimum period of one-half hour.
pending
202X1994OIML R 113:1994
#01495
3.8
a measure of the variation in the detector’s output signal when a sample component is not being detected. This can be classified into two types: Short-term noise includes all observable random variations of the signal from the detector or other components having a frequency of about one or more cycles per minute. Drift is the average slope of the baseline signal measured over an extended time period, for example, one-half hour
pending
